title: Thai Fish Cakes

ingredients.name: white fish fillets (tilapia, pollock or any firm white fish), roughly chopped

subs.name: 400 g peeled prawns, blended, for a prawn cake version

ingredients.name: red curry paste (store-bought)
visual_checks: store-bought Thai red curry paste; mix of chilli powder and spices approximates it
ingredients.notes: store-bought Thai red curry paste; mix of chilli powder and spices approximates it

subs.name: 1 tbsp red chilli paste or a mix of 1 tsp chilli powder, 1 tsp lemongrass paste and 0.5 tsp turmeric

ingredients.name: kaffir lime leaves, very finely sliced into thin strips

subs.name: 0.5 tsp finely grated lime zest mixed into the paste

ingredients.name: green beans, thinly sliced into 3 mm rounds

subs.name: finely sliced spring onion or grated carrot

steps.text: Make the cucumber relish first so it has time to meld: combine the diced cucumber with vinegar, sugar and a pinch of salt in a small bowl. Stir until the sugar dissolves. Add crushed peanuts if using. Set aside at room temperature.

Place the fish pieces in a food processor or mixie. Blend to a rough paste — you want it sticky but not completely smooth; some texture is good. Add the red curry paste, fish sauce, egg, sugar and half the kaffir lime leaf strips. Pulse a few times to combine.

Transfer the fish paste to a bowl. Add the sliced green beans and the remaining kaffir lime leaf strips. Mix well by hand — the mixture should be sticky enough to hold its shape when pressed into a patty. If too wet, refrigerate for 15 minutes.
With wet hands, shape the mixture into flat round patties about 6 cm wide and 1 cm thick. You should get about 12-14 pieces. Wet hands prevent sticking and help shape the patties smoothly.

Heat the oil in a flat-bottomed pan or tawa over medium heat. When the oil shimmers, add the fish cakes in a single layer without crowding. Fry for 3-4 minutes per side until deep golden and cooked through — press one gently with a spatula; it should feel firm, not squishy.
Drain on paper towel for 1 minute. Serve hot with the cucumber relish on the side for dipping. Fish cakes are best eaten immediately while crisp; they soften as they cool.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
Keep water away from hot oil, lower food gently, avoid overfilling the vessel and never leave frying unattended.
critical_checks: Cook seafood until safely done for the species and preparation; keep it chilled before cooking.
Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: fish → 400 g peeled prawns, blended, for a prawn cake version; red-chilli-powder → 1 tbsp red chilli paste or a mix of 1 tsp chilli powder, 1 tsp lemongrass paste and 0.5 tsp turmeric (approximate the curry paste flavour); kaffir-lime-leaves → 0.5 tsp finely grated lime zest mixed into the paste; beans → finely sliced spring onion or grated carrot (for crunch and colour)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Use a flat tawa or non-stick pan for even shallow-frying. Any firm white fish available in Indian markets works well — tilapia (tilapia), seer fish (vanjaram) or pomfret all give good results. Red curry paste can be approximated with a mixture of Kashmiri chilli paste, lemongrass paste (available as a tube in specialty stores) and a little ginger-garlic paste. A mixie blends the fish paste perfectly.
